title: Client Instance Assertion — SPIFFE Workload (SPIRE → OAuth)
description: A Kubernetes pod attested by SPIRE fetches a JWT-SVID from the SPIFFE Workload API, then exchanges it at a SPIFFE-OAuth adapter (Instance Issuer) to receive a re-minted client-instance+jwt. The re-minted assertion adds cnf.jkt (DPoP key binding), client_id, and the required typ — bridging SPIFFE workload identity into OAuth sender-constrained tokens.

steps.label: FetchJWTSVID (Workload API)
steps.explanation: The pod calls the SPIFFE Workload API over a UNIX domain socket (no credentials needed — the SPIRE Agent attests the caller via kernel-level process inspection). It requests a JWT-SVID bound to the Instance Issuer's URL as audience. The SPIRE Agent contacts the SPIRE Server to fetch a valid SVID for this workload's SPIFFE ID.
annotations: The SPIFFE Workload API is a gRPC API exposed by the SPIRE Agent over a UNIX domain socket (default: /run/spire/sockets/agent.sock).
Attestation is transparent — the SPIRE Agent authenticates the caller using OS-level signals (Linux namespaces, cgroups, pod UID). No credentials are passed in the request.
The audience list MUST match the Instance Issuer's identifier. Presenting this SVID to any other service will fail audience validation.
SPIRE issues SVIDs backed by the SPIRE Server's CA — the trust bundle (root CA certs) is what the Issuer uses to verify the SVID signature.

steps.label: JWT-SVID Response

artifacts.signing_key_hint: SPIRE Server CA signing key (kid: spire-signing-key-abc123) — not the workload's key

steps.label: 1. JOSE Header
steps.value:
{
  "alg": "ES256",
  "kid": "spire-signing-key-abc123",
  "typ": "JWT"
}

CRITICAL: typ is plain 'JWT' — not 'client-instance+jwt'.
This is a raw SPIFFE JWT-SVID, NOT a Client Instance Assertion.

The Instance Issuer will:
  1. Validate this SVID (SPIRE CA trust bundle)
  2. Re-mint it as typ='client-instance+jwt'
  3. Add cnf.jkt and client_id (absent from raw SVIDs)

The SPIRE Server's signing key is SEPARATE from the
workload's own EC key pair used for DPoP.

steps.label: 2. JWT-SVID Payload
steps.value:
{
  "iss": "spiffe://openai.example.com",
  "sub": "spiffe://openai.example.com/codex/pod-abc123",
  "aud": ["https://issuer.openai.example.com"],
  "iat": 1770000000,
  "exp": 1770000300
}

iss  — SPIFFE trust domain URI (not an HTTPS URL)
sub  — the workload's SPIFFE ID (unique within trust domain)
aud  — Instance Issuer URL; prevents replay at any other service

NOTICE what is ABSENT in a raw JWT-SVID:
  ✗ No client_id  — SPIRE knows nothing about OAuth clients
  ✗ No cnf        — SPIRE signs with its own CA, not the workload key
  ✗ No sub_profile — SPIRE is not OAuth-aware
  ✗ No jti        — raw SVIDs may omit jti (§8.8.1 replay exemption)
  ✗ No typ=client-instance+jwt

The Instance Issuer bridges these gaps in the next step.

steps.label: 3. SPIRE Trust Bundle Verification
steps.value:
The Instance Issuer validates the SVID using:

  SPIFFE Trust Bundle (from SPIRE Server):
  {
    "trust_domain": "openai.example.com",
    "jwt_authorities": [
      {
        "public_key_type": "EC",
        "key": "<SPIRE Server CA public key>",
        "key_id": "spire-signing-key-abc123",
        "expires_at": "..."
      }
    ]
  }

The Issuer fetches this bundle from:
  SPIRE: https://spire-server.openai.example.com/bundle
  OR: via SPIFFE Federation (spiffe_bundle_endpoint)

Validation checks:
  1. alg ∈ allowed algorithms (ES256) ✓
  2. kid matches a jwt_authorities entry ✓
  3. Signature verifies against CA public key ✓
  4. sub falls within trust_domain openai.example.com ✓
  5. sub satisfies spiffe_id pattern: spiffe://openai.example.com/codex/* ✓
  6. aud = issuer's own URL ✓
  7. exp not expired ✓

steps.explanation: The SPIRE Agent returns a short-lived JWT-SVID signed by the SPIRE Server's CA. This is a raw SPIFFE credential — it proves the workload's SPIFFE ID but carries none of the OAuth claims (client_id, cnf.jkt, sub_profile) required by the Client Instance Assertion spec. The next step bridges SPIFFE identity into the OAuth world.
annotations: SPIFFE JWT-SVIDs use typ='JWT', NOT 'client-instance+jwt'. They cannot be used directly as actor_token without SPIFFE compat mode (§8.8).
The SVID is signed by the SPIRE Server's CA — a completely different key from the workload's own EC key pair used for DPoP.
SPIRE SVIDs typically have short TTLs (300–3600 seconds) and are automatically rotated by the SPIRE Agent.
§8.8.1: Raw JWT-SVIDs CAN be used directly as actor_token if the descriptor uses spiffe_bundle_endpoint and no client_id claim is needed. This scenario shows the more common re-minting path.

steps.label: POST /assert (SVID exchange)
steps.explanation: The workload presents its JWT-SVID along with its EC public key (JWK) to the SPIFFE-OAuth Adapter (Instance Issuer). The workload generated this EC key pair at startup — it is the instance's DPoP key. The Issuer validates the SVID using the SPIFFE trust bundle, checks the SPIFFE ID against authorized patterns, then re-mints a client-instance+jwt that includes cnf.jkt and client_id.
annotations: The workload's EC public key is provided here so the Issuer can embed its SHA-256 thumbprint as cnf.jkt in the re-minted assertion.
The workload holds the private half of this EC key pair and uses it to sign all DPoP proofs — this is the key-binding thread that runs through the entire chain.
The Issuer is a trusted intermediary: it knows which SPIFFE IDs are authorized to claim which client_ids. This authorization mapping is an operator configuration.
client_id and audience are explicit request parameters — the Issuer will only issue an assertion if the SPIFFE ID is authorized for that client_id.

steps.label: client-instance+jwt

artifacts.signing_key_hint: Instance Issuer ES256 key (kid: 4vC8agycHu6rnkEEJYAH6VuCe4JoSkPV) — NOT the SPIRE CA key

steps.label: 1. JOSE Header (Re-minted)
steps.value:
{
  "alg": "ES256",
  "kid": "4vC8agycHu6rnkEEJYAH6VuCe4JoSkPV",
  "typ": "client-instance+jwt"
}

KEY TRANSFORMATION: typ changed from 'JWT' → 'client-instance+jwt'

This is NOT the SPIRE CA key. The Issuer signs with its own
OAuth-registered key. The AS verifies using the Issuer's jwks_uri,
not the SPIRE trust bundle.

Client metadata descriptor:
{
  "issuer": "https://issuer.openai.example.com",
  "jwks_uri": "https://issuer.openai.example.com/jwks.json",  ← Issuer's key
  "subject_syntax": "spiffe",
  "trust_domain": "openai.example.com",
  "spiffe_id": "spiffe://openai.example.com/codex/*",
  "signing_alg_values_supported": ["ES256"]
}

steps.label: 2. Re-minted Payload (gaps filled)
steps.value:
{
  "iss": "https://issuer.openai.example.com",    ← OAuth HTTPS URL (not spiffe://)
  "sub": "spiffe://openai.example.com/codex/pod-abc123",  ← preserved from SVID
  "aud": "https://as.example.com",              ← OAuth AS (not the issuer)
  "client_id": "https://openai.example.com/codex",  ← ADDED (absent in raw SVID)
  "iat": 1770000010,
  "nbf": 1770000010,
  "exp": 1770000310,
  "jti": "8f3e2a1b-9c4d",                       ← ADDED for replay prevention
  "cnf": { "jkt": "0ZcOCORZNYyOWyYiguA4I" },   ← ADDED (workload's DPoP key)
  "sub_profile": "client_instance"              ← ADDED
}

THE THREE GAPS FILLED BY RE-MINTING:
  1. client_id — maps SPIFFE ID to registered OAuth client
  2. cnf.jkt  — binds the workload's DPoP EC key (from request)
  3. typ      — changed to client-instance+jwt for AS type-safety

SECURITY: The Issuer checked that
  spiffe://openai.example.com/codex/pod-abc123
  matches descriptor spiffe_id: spiffe://openai.example.com/codex/*
  before authorizing client_id=https://openai.example.com/codex

steps.label: 3. Key Chain: SPIRE CA → Issuer → Workload
steps.value:
Three independent key pairs in this flow:

1. SPIRE CA signing key (kid: spire-signing-key-abc123)
   Signs the JWT-SVID
   Verified using: SPIFFE trust bundle (SPIRE CA cert)
   Scope: proves pod identity to SPIFFE-aware services

2. Instance Issuer key (kid: 4vC8agycHu6rnkEEJYAH6VuCe4JoSkPV)
   Signs the re-minted client-instance+jwt
   Verified using: AS fetches Issuer's jwks_uri
   Scope: OAuth token endpoint validation

3. Workload EC key (JWK thumbprint: 0ZcOCORZNYyOWyYiguA4I)
   NOT used for signing tokens — used for DPoP proofs
   Verified using: JWK thumbprint comparison (cnf.jkt)
   Scope: sender-constraint binding (step 5 → step 7)

Only key #3 follows the workload all the way to the RS.

steps.explanation: The SPIFFE-OAuth Adapter re-mints the JWT-SVID as a proper client-instance+jwt. The critical transformation: client_id and cnf.jkt are added (absent from raw SVIDs), the typ is set correctly, and the token is signed by the Issuer's OAuth-registered key rather than the SPIRE CA. The AS can now process this token using standard Client Instance Assertion validation — no SPIFFE-awareness required.
annotations: §7.3: The re-minted token has typ='client-instance+jwt'. The raw SVID had typ='JWT' — the AS would reject the raw SVID as an actor_token unless using §8.8 SPIFFE compat mode.
The Issuer's iss is an HTTPS URL registered in instance_issuers. SPIFFE iss URIs (spiffe://) are only used in raw SVID compat mode (§8.8.2).
cnf.jkt binds the workload's own EC key — distinct from both the SPIRE CA key and the Issuer's signing key.
§12.2: The sub (SPIFFE ID) is stable and unique per pod. When SPIRE rotates the SVID, the SPIFFE ID stays the same — only the signing changes.

steps.label: POST /token (client_creds)

artifacts.signing_key_hint: Workload EC private key — the key whose thumbprint is cnf.jkt in the assertion
artifacts.final_output: DPoP proof tying the token request to the workload's EC key

steps.label: 1. DPoP Proof Header
steps.value:
{
  "typ": "dpop+jwt",
  "alg": "ES256",
  "jwk": {
    "kty": "EC",
    "x": "EC_pub_x",
    "y": "EC_pub_y",
    "crv": "P-256"
  }
}

The jwk embeds the workload's public key inline.
SHA-256 thumbprint of this JWK = 0ZcOCORZNYyOWyYiguA4I

The AS computes: SHA-256(canonicalized JWK) and verifies
it equals the cnf.jkt in the actor_token.
This proves: the party presenting the assertion also controls
the private key — not just a replay of a stolen assertion.

steps.label: 2. Full Token Request
steps.value:
grant_type=client_credentials
&scope=repo.write
&client_id=https://openai.example.com/codex
&actor_token=<Re-minted client-instance+jwt from step 4>
&actor_token_type=
  urn:ietf:params:oauth:token-type:client-instance-jwt

DPoP: <proof in header — workload EC key matching cnf.jkt>

AS Validation (§8.2 for client_instance_jwt auth):
  1. Read actor_token iss = https://issuer.openai.example.com
  2. Look up descriptor in client metadata instance_issuers
  3. Fetch jwks_uri = https://issuer.openai.example.com/jwks.json
  4. Verify ES256 sig on client-instance+jwt ✓
  5. Validate typ='client-instance+jwt' ✓
  6. Validate sub ∈ trust_domain openai.example.com ✓
  7. Validate sub satisfies spiffe_id pattern ✓
  8. Verify client_id in assertion = client_id in request ✓
  9. Check (iss, jti) replay cache: 8f3e2a1b-9c4d not seen ✓
  10. Verify DPoP JWK thumbprint = cnf.jkt ✓
  11. §8.6.1: client_credentials → SELF-ACTING case

steps.explanation: The workload presents the re-minted client-instance+jwt as its actor_token. Using the client_instance_jwt auth method, no separate client_assertion is needed. The DPoP proof uses the same EC private key whose public key thumbprint is embedded as cnf.jkt in the assertion — the AS verifies this linkage to prevent assertion replay.
annotations: §8.3.1: client_instance_jwt auth method — actor_token serves as both client authentication and instance identification.
§8.5: AS MUST verify DPoP JWK thumbprint = cnf.jkt in assertion. MUST NOT issue bearer token.
§8.2 Step 6: AS checks sub against descriptor's trust_domain and spiffe_id constraints from client metadata.
actor_token_type = urn:ietf:params:oauth:token-type:client-instance-jwt

steps.label: 200 OK + Access Token

artifacts.final_output: DPoP-bound Access Token (SPIFFE ID as sub, self-acting)

steps.label: 1. Access Token (Self-Acting, §8.6.3)
steps.value:
{
  "iss": "https://as.example.com",
  "aud": "https://api.example.com",
  "sub": "spiffe://openai.example.com/codex/pod-abc123",
  "sub_profile": "client_instance",
  "client_id": "https://openai.example.com/codex",
  "scope": "repo.write",
  "cnf": { "jkt": "0ZcOCORZNYyOWyYiguA4I" },
  "iat": 1770000020,
  "exp": 1770003600
}

SPIFFE ID survives into the access token as sub:
  spiffe://openai.example.com/codex/pod-abc123

Resource servers can use this SPIFFE ID for:
  • Workload-to-workload authorization policy
  • Audit logging (which specific pod made the request)
  • SPIRE-aware policy engines (OPA, Envoy RBAC)

steps.label: 2. Full Key Binding Chain
steps.value:
End-to-end key binding across all 4 tokens:

  SPIFFE JWT-SVID (step 2)
  └─ signed by: SPIRE CA (spire-signing-key-abc123)
  └─ proves: pod identity to SPIFFE ecosystem

  Re-minted client-instance+jwt (step 4)
  └─ signed by: Instance Issuer (4vC8agycHu6rnk...)
  └─ cnf.jkt = 0ZcOCORZNYy... (workload EC key)

  DPoP proof at /token (step 5)
  └─ signed by: Workload EC key
  └─ JWK thumbprint = cnf.jkt ✓ (AS verified)

  Access token (this step)
  └─ signed by: AS (HTTPS RS256/ES256)
  └─ cnf.jkt = 0ZcOCORZNYy... (same workload key)

  DPoP proof at /api/repos (step 7)
  └─ signed by: Workload EC key
  └─ JWK thumbprint = cnf.jkt in access token ✓ (RS verified)

The workload's EC key is the red thread binding all 5 steps.
The SPIRE CA key and Issuer key drop out after step 4.

steps.explanation: The AS issues a DPoP-bound access token with the SPIFFE ID as sub. The SPIFFE ID is now a first-class OAuth principal — resource servers can use it for SPIFFE-aware policy without any knowledge of SPIRE or the trust bundle. The workload's EC key is bound via cnf.jkt, completing the sender-constraint chain.
annotations: §8.6.3: Self-acting case — sub = actor_token's sub (the SPIFFE ID). act MUST be omitted.
§9.1: RS sees sub_profile='client_instance' — applies workload-class policy, not user-scoped policy.
The SPIFFE ID in sub is stable across SVID rotations. Token revocation by SPIFFE ID (§10.1) is meaningful for this reason.
§8.5: token_type='DPoP'. The RS must verify DPoP proof on every request — the access token alone is insufficient without the workload's private key.

steps.label: GET /api/repos (DPoP-bound)

artifacts.signing_key_hint: Workload EC private key (same key as step 5; jwk thumbprint = cnf.jkt in access token)
artifacts.final_output: DPoP proof for GET /api/repos — binds this specific request to the workload EC key

steps.label: 1. DPoP Proof Header (RFC 9449 §4.2)
steps.value:
{
  "typ": "dpop+jwt",
  "alg": "ES256",
  "jwk": {
    "kty": "EC",
    "x": "EC_pub_x",
    "y": "EC_pub_y",
    "crv": "P-256"
  }
}

The jwk MUST appear in the JOSE header (not the payload).
This is the workload's public key — the RS extracts it
to verify the proof signature.

RS then checks:
  SHA-256(canonicalized jwk) == cnf.jkt in access token
  i.e. 0ZcOCORZNYyOWyYiguA4I == 0ZcOCORZNYyOWyYiguA4I ✓

This proves the presenter holds the private key bound
to the access token — not a replay of a stolen token.

steps.label: 2. DPoP Proof Payload (RS-side, RFC 9449 §4.3)
steps.value:
{
  "jti": "dpop-spiffe-002",
  "htm": "GET",
  "htu": "https://api.example.com/api/repos",
  "iat": 1770000500,
  "ath": "HU1wAEu28wMDcaJIyQlV1Xi6O7jKLeTprBrvYfBtFlo"
}

jti  — unique per proof; RS caches to reject replays
htm  — HTTP method (GET); RS rejects if it doesn't match
htu  — request URI; RS rejects if it doesn't match
iat  — issued recently; RS rejects if too old (§11.1)
ath  — BASE64URL(SHA-256(access_token_string)) ← REQUIRED at RS

ath binds this DPoP proof to exactly this access token.
Without ath, a stolen proof could be replayed with a
different access token. RFC 9449 §4.3: ath MUST be
present when the DPoP proof is used at a RS.

NOTE: ath was NOT included at the token endpoint (step 5)
because no access token existed yet at that point.

steps.label: 3. RS Validation Steps (RFC 9449 §7.1)
steps.value:
The RS validates the DPoP proof as follows:

  1. Parse DPoP header from JOSE header (not payload)
  2. Verify typ = "dpop+jwt" ✓
  3. Verify alg ∈ supported algorithms ✓
  4. Extract jwk from JOSE header ✓
  5. Verify proof signature using extracted jwk ✓
  6. Verify htm = request HTTP method (GET) ✓
  7. Verify htu = request URI ✓
  8. Verify iat is recent (within allowed drift) ✓
  9. Check jti not in replay cache → add to cache ✓
  10. Compute SHA-256(access_token) → BASE64URL encode
      = HU1wAEu28wMDcaJIyQlV1Xi6O7jKLeTprBrvYfBtFlo
      Compare to ath in proof ✓
  11. Compute SHA-256(canonicalized jwk from proof)
      = 0ZcOCORZNYyOWyYiguA4I
      Compare to cnf.jkt in access token ✓

All checks pass → request authorized as:
  sub: spiffe://openai.example.com/codex/pod-abc123
  scope: repo.write

steps.explanation: The workload calls the resource server with its DPoP-bound access token. The RS validates the access token and DPoP proof without any knowledge of SPIRE — it sees only a standard OAuth access token with a SPIFFE-format sub. The RS can use sub_profile='client_instance' to apply workload-specific authorization policy.
annotations: The RS has no SPIRE dependency — it sees a standard at+jwt. The SPIFFE identity is just a string in the sub claim.
§9.1: RS evaluates sub_profile='client_instance' to determine this is a workload (not a user) and applies appropriate policy.
DPoP proof binds ath (access token hash) to prevent the proof from being reused with a different token.
The workload's EC key has been the constant sender-constraint anchor from step 3 (SVID exchange) all the way to this RS call.

steps.label: 200 OK + Resource
steps.explanation: The resource server returns the data. The full trust chain ran: SPIRE attested the pod → SPIFFE-OAuth adapter bridged to OAuth → AS issued a DPoP-bound token → RS enforced sender-constraint. Three separate trust anchors (SPIRE CA, Instance Issuer, AS) each played their role, but only the workload's EC key ran the whole race.
annotations: authorized_workload in the response illustrates how an RS can log the SPIFFE ID for forensic audit — the same pod identity that SPIRE manages.
§10.1: The AS can revoke all tokens for this SPIFFE ID (pod-abc123) independently of other pods in the same client.
When SPIRE rotates the pod's SVID, the workload repeats steps 1–4 to get a fresh assertion. The access token TTL (3600s) is typically longer than the SVID TTL (300s) — this is intentional.
Full SPIFFE→OAuth trust delegation: SPIRE attests identity → Issuer translates it → AS issues OAuth tokens → RS enforces policy.
